content.text: Streaming responses let an LLM API send its answer token by token as they are generated, rather than waiting until the full response is ready. The result is a dramatically shorter perceived latency: the user sees text appearing in near real-time instead of staring at a spinner for seconds or minutes.

A streaming response is an HTTP response whose body is sent incrementally over a single long-lived connection rather than in one complete payload. The server writes chunks of data as soon as they are available and the client processes each chunk immediately. For LLM APIs the chunks are individual tokens (or small groups of tokens), typically formatted as
When you call the OpenAI, Anthropic, or similar APIs with
, the server does not buffer the entire completion. Instead it emits a delta event for every token it samples. Your client code processes each event in an
listener (Node.js), appending each delta to the accumulated output.

LLM inference is slow by the standards of web APIs. A long answer can take tens of seconds to generate. Without streaming, the user sees nothing until every token is ready — a blank screen that erodes trust and feels broken. With streaming, the first token arrives within hundreds of milliseconds and the user immediately sees progress.

Streaming also matters for agent systems. A multi-step agent may need to show intermediate reasoning steps, tool call arguments, or partial sub-answers before the final response is assembled. Streaming is the mechanism that makes all of that visible in real time.
— users see the first token in milliseconds, not after the full TTFT (time to first byte)
— markdown, code blocks, and structured content can be rendered as tokens arrive
— the user can abort a response the moment they see it going in the wrong direction, saving API cost
— streaming keeps the HTTP connection alive even during slow generation, avoiding gateway timeouts
— neither client nor proxy needs to buffer the entire response in memory before processing starts
— intermediate reasoning tokens and tool-call deltas can be shown to the user as the agent works
— you must accumulate deltas, handle partial JSON, and manage connection lifecycle
— some HTTP proxies buffer entire responses; you must configure them to flush immediately
— errors can arrive mid-stream after some tokens have already been shown to the user
— mocking a streaming endpoint requires async generator or SSE test helpers
— if you need to transform or validate the full response before showing anything, streaming complicates the pipeline
Autoregressive generation samples one token at a time. After sampling token
, the LLM engine appends it to the context and samples token
. Because each token is independently available the moment it is sampled, the inference server can forward it to the HTTP response buffer immediately rather than accumulating all tokens first.
On the wire, most LLM APIs use Server-Sent Events (SSE). Each token delta is sent as a
line in the SSE stream, terminated by a double newline. The final event is
, which signals that generation is complete. Some APIs (e.g. Anthropic) use named event types (
) to distinguish token deltas from metadata events.
The diagram below shows the token-by-token flow from LLM engine to client UI. The client sends a single HTTP POST with
. The gateway forwards the request to the LLM engine and proxies SSE chunks back in real time. The browser UI appends each token delta as it arrives, producing the typewriter effect.
— any UI where a user waits for an LLM reply benefits from the typewriter effect
— documents, essays, code files that take more than 5 seconds to generate
— showing chain-of-thought thinking steps as they are computed
— when users need the ability to stop generation early to save cost or time
— streaming lets you display tool arguments and call results as the agent constructs them
Batch jobs where the caller stores the response in a database — non-streaming is simpler to parse and retry
When you need the full response for validation before showing anything to the user (e.g., strict JSON extraction)
When the environment does not support HTTP streaming (some PaaS functions have a fixed response model)

Implementation has two sides: the client that calls the LLM API with streaming enabled, and the server (if you build a proxy or BFF) that passes the stream through to your own frontend. Both patterns are covered below.
Client-side: consuming a streaming LLM API

"""Minimal streaming client for OpenAI-compatible APIs.

Runs standalone: python streaming_client.py
"""
import os
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"])


def stream_chat(prompt: str) -> str:
    """Stream a completion and return the full accumulated text."""
    accumulated: list[str] = []

    with client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        stream=True,          # enable streaming
        max_tokens=512,
    ) as stream:
        for chunk in stream:
            delta = chunk.choices[0].delta
            if delta.content is not None:
                print(delta.content, end="", flush=True)
                accumulated.append(delta.content)

            # finish_reason signals end of generation
            if chunk.choices[0].finish_reason == "stop":
                break

    print()  # newline after stream ends
    return "".join(accumulated)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    full_text = stream_chat("Explain streaming responses in two sentences.")
    print(f"\n[Total words in response: {len(full_text.split())}]")
Server-side: proxying a stream through FastAPI

"""FastAPI backend that proxies streaming LLM responses to the browser.

Start: uvicorn streaming_proxy:app --reload
Test: curl -N http://localhost:8000/chat?q=hello
"""
import json
import os
from collections.abc import AsyncIterator

from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

app = FastAPI()
aclient = AsyncOpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"])


async def llm_token_generator(prompt: str) -> AsyncIterator[str]:
    """Yield SSE-formatted chunks as the LLM generates tokens."""
    async with aclient.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        stream=True,
        max_tokens=256,
    ) as stream:
        async for chunk in stream:
            delta = chunk.choices[0].delta
            if delta.content:
                payload = json.dumps({"token": delta.content})
                yield f"data: {payload}\n\n"
            if chunk.choices[0].finish_reason is not None:
                yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"
                return


@app.get("/chat")
async def chat_stream(
    q: str = Query(..., description="User prompt"),
) -> StreamingResponse:
    return StreamingResponse(
        llm_token_generator(q),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",   # disable nginx buffering
            "Connection": "keep-alive",
        },
    )


@app.get("/health")
async def health() -> dict:
    return {"status": "ok"}
Anthropic's API uses named SSE event types. The
signals completion. The Python SDK wraps this so the loop looks similar to OpenAI's.

import anthropic
import os

client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])

# stream=True is the default when using the streaming context manager
with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
    max_tokens=256,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is streaming in LLMs?"}],
) as stream:
    for text_chunk in stream.text_stream:
        print(text_chunk, end="", flush=True)

print()  # newline
# stream.get_final_message() returns the complete Message object
final = stream.get_final_message()
print(f"Stop reason: {final.stop_reason}")
print(f"Input tokens: {final.usage.input_tokens}")
print(f"Output tokens: {final.usage.output_tokens}")

"""Track streaming progress and cost estimate in real time."""
import os
from openai import OpenAI

OPENAI_OUTPUT_COST_PER_1K = 0.00015  # gpt-4o-mini output, USD per 1k tokens

client = OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"])


def stream_with_stats(prompt: str) -> None:
    accumulated: list[str] = []

    with client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        stream=True,
        stream_options={"include_usage": True},  # request usage in final chunk
    ) as stream:
        for chunk in stream:
            choice = chunk.choices[0] if chunk.choices else None
            if choice and choice.delta.content:
                text = choice.delta.content
                print(text, end="", flush=True)
                accumulated.append(text)

            # Final chunk carries authoritative usage data
            if chunk.usage:
                output_tokens = chunk.usage.completion_tokens
                cost = output_tokens * OPENAI_OUTPUT_COST_PER_1K / 1000
                print(
                    f"\n\nOutput tokens: {output_tokens}"
                    f" | Est. cost: ${cost:.6f}"
                )


if __name__ == "__main__":
    stream_with_stats("List three benefits of streaming LLM responses.")

ChatGPT's web interface uses streaming. When you send a message, the browser opens a fetch connection to OpenAI's API and appends tokens to the chat bubble as they arrive. The perceived response time is under one second from submit click to first token, even though the full reply might take 15 seconds to complete. Without streaming, the user would see nothing for those 15 seconds and the experience would feel broken.

Cursor (the AI code editor) streams multi-step edits as the model generates them. Each token of the diff appears in the editor inline, so the developer can already start reviewing the first proposed change while the model is still generating later ones.

Perplexity AI streams citations and sources mid-answer. As the LLM produces the text it also emits structured metadata events that tell the UI which citation to highlight next to the currently generated sentence. This demonstrates that streaming events do not have to carry only raw token text — they can carry any structured data that the client needs to render progress.
— nginx and some WSGI servers buffer output by default. Set
(nginx) to prevent unexpected buffering of your SSE stream.
Parsing only the first SSE line per chunk
— a single TCP packet can carry multiple
and process each event in the loop.
— not checking for the done marker causes the parser to try to deserialise the literal string
as JSON and throw a parse error.
Holding a database connection open during streaming
— if your handler keeps a DB transaction open while the stream is in flight, you exhaust the connection pool. Write the final result after
— the API can return an error event after sending some tokens. Wrap the loop in
and emit an error state to the UI if the stream breaks.
Using synchronous I/O in an async server route
FastAPI route blocks the event loop. Use
on every SSE response to prevent intermediary caching and premature closure
Add a heartbeat ping every 15 to 30 seconds on long streams to prevent load balancers from closing idle connections
Validate the full accumulated text after
if downstream logic depends on well-formed JSON or structured output
Persist the full final response to a database only after the stream completes — never write partial tokens row by row
Use a correlation ID (request ID) in each SSE event so logs can be joined even when multiple streams run concurrently
(Python) so abandoned streams do not keep burning API credits

Streaming shifts the performance bottleneck from network transfer to token generation speed (tokens per second). A model generating 50 tokens/s will produce visible output for 20 seconds for a 1000-token response regardless of streaming — streaming just makes each of those seconds productive for the reader.

Each active stream holds one open HTTP connection. At scale this can exhaust file descriptors or thread pools if you use a threaded WSGI server. An async ASGI server (Uvicorn + FastAPI, or Starlette) handles thousands of concurrent streams with a small number of OS threads because each stream yields control during the
Each stream occupies one worker thread throughout
Coroutines yield between token reads; no blocking
Event-loop model same as async Python
— built-in ASGI response type that accepts an async generator
on any runnable; streaming events include tool-call deltas
and React hooks for streaming UI out of the box
supports SSE without the OpenAI SDK for custom endpoints
Long-lived HTTP connections introduce a larger attack surface than short requests. Enforce the following safeguards:
— validate tokens or API keys in the HTTP headers before the generator yields its first byte. Never authenticate mid-stream.
— cap the maximum stream duration (e.g. 120 s) to prevent resource exhaustion from abandoned or slow clients.
— a single user opening hundreds of concurrent streams will deplete your API quota and file descriptors.
Do not log raw token deltas at DEBUG level in production
— user-generated content and model output can contain sensitive data; log aggregated metrics instead.
Detect and handle broken pipes gracefully
— when the client disconnects mid-stream, stop the generator and release any held resources (locks, DB cursors, API credits).
field on each event; if the client reconnects it sends
so the server can replay missed events.
Circuit breaker around the upstream LLM call
— if the LLM API is down, fail fast and return an error SSE event instead of hanging the connection.
— generate a unique run ID per stream request so repeated retries can be deduplicated in your logs and billing.

Think of streaming as reading from a pipe rather than waiting for a file. The writer (LLM engine) and reader (your client) run simultaneously — the writer puts tokens into the pipe and the reader takes them out in real time. Neither has to wait for the other to finish.

A non-streaming call is equivalent to waiting for the entire file to be written before you are allowed to open it. Streaming opens the file handle immediately and reads bytes as they arrive. The total time to read everything is the same — only the experience of waiting changes.
Streaming sends tokens incrementally over a single long-lived HTTP connection using SSE or chunked transfer encoding
The primary benefit is perceived latency reduction — the user sees the first token in milliseconds, not after the full response is ready
in the Anthropic SDK; iterate the resulting async generator in a loop
Use an async ASGI server (FastAPI + Uvicorn) to serve many concurrent streams without thread exhaustion
Accumulate deltas in memory and persist to the database only after
— never write partial tokens to storage
Always handle mid-stream errors and broken pipes; avoid leaving API calls or DB connections open after the client disconnects
For batch jobs or structured extraction that requires the full response before proceeding, plain non-streaming calls are simpler and equally correct
